title: API Security
objectives: Apply authN, authZ and transport security
Prevent the OWASP API Top 10 classics
Add rate limiting, CORS and input validation

sections.markdown: API security is a stack: HTTPS everywhere, strong authentication (JWT/OAuth/mTLS), granular authorization on every endpoint, input validation, rate limiting, and monitoring for abuse.

The #1 API bug in the wild is **BOLA/IDOR**: `GET /orders/42` returns any order because the server trusts the URL id. Fix: always check that the requester owns/can-access the resource, not just that they're logged in.
Client-only validation. Attackers bypass your app entirely; the server must revalidate every input. Also: putting secrets/tokens in URLs (they leak via logs and Referer).
HTTPS (HSTS), short-lived JWTs, refresh-token rotation, per-user rate limits (token bucket), strict CORS allowlist, security headers, structured audit logging, and secret scanning in CI.

flashcards.back: Broken Object-Level Authorization: the server acts on a client-supplied ID without checking ownership.
Always on the server, regardless of client validation.
Token bucket or sliding window, keyed per user/IP.

quiz.q: The most common API vulnerability is…
quiz.explain: OWASP API Top 10 #1: server trusts client-supplied IDs.

quiz.q: Rate limits are typically implemented with…
quiz.explain: Token bucket allows bursts but bounds sustained rate.
Never ship secrets to clients or logs.

challenges.text: Add HTTPS + HSTS to a service.
Add per-user token-bucket rate limiting; return 429 + Retry-After.
Fix a BOLA bug: enforce ownership on GET /orders/:id.
Set up short-lived JWTs + refresh rotation + revocation list.

interviewQuestions: Name the OWASP API Top 10 and give one fix each.
What is BOLA/IDOR and how do you prevent it?
How do you rate-limit an API?
How do you handle secrets in a backend?
How do you audit and monitor API security?
